findings.message: TASK-03 still specifies the wrong self-invocation mechanism. The acceptance says the `scheduled` handler should call `fetch(new Request("https://localhost/api/internal/inbox-recovery", ...))`, but the generated OpenNext worker exports a default object with a `fetch(request, env, ctx)` method at `apps/reception/.open-next/worker.js:15-46`. Global `fetch()` to `https://localhost/...` is not the same as delegating to that handler and does not use the worker's own `env`/`ctx` contract. The wrapper needs to call the imported OpenNext handler directly, not an external-looking localhost request.

TASK-02 says the new `recovery.server.ts` module will mirror the full `syncInbox` draft generation context, but the key helpers it cites are currently local, non-exported functions in `apps/reception/src/lib/inbox/sync.server.ts:156`, `apps/reception/src/lib/inbox/sync.server.ts:179`, and `apps/reception/src/lib/inbox/sync.server.ts:192`. As planned, the new module cannot reuse `buildThreadContext`, `inferPrepaymentProvider`, or `inferPrepaymentStep` without first extracting shared helpers or duplicating logic.

The retry-counter mitigation is not backed by the current metadata behavior. The plan says `recoveryAttempts` resets on next sync if thread state changes, but `buildThreadMetadata()` in `apps/reception/src/lib/inbox/sync.server.ts:242-258` starts with `...existing`, so unknown metadata keys persist unless explicitly cleared. The plan never defines a reset path, which means retry debt can leak forward across later syncs.

The confidence math is still internally inconsistent. The calculation block rounds the overall score to `80%` at line 319, but the header reports `Overall-confidence: 83%` and line 320 says "Reported as 83%."
